subject: C S
title: Introduction to Computer Programming
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to object-oriented program design and development.  Principles of algorithm formulation and implementation.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Understand the importance of the following concepts
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: AbstractionDebugging and Assurance of CorrectnessProgram Development (analysis, specification, design, implementation, testing)

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Write programs whose complexity reaches a few hundred lines

subject: C S
title: Academic Internship
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Internships or cooperative education experiences with organizations outside BYU.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Provide Work Experience
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will develop familiarity with industrial software development and analysis.

subject: C S
title: Algorithm Design and Analysis
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: A study of the design and analysis of algorithms as solutions to problems, including dynamic programming, linear programming, greedy algorithms, divide-and-conquer algorithms, graph algorithms, and intelligent search algorithms.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Efficiency
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: The student can make the distinction between problems and their algorithmic solutions. Moreover, the student can distinguish between efficient and inefficient solutions and can relate these distinctions to the complexity classes of problems, namely P, NP, and NP-Complete learned earlier in the curriculum (CS 252).

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Fundamental Computer Algorithms
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: The student understands fundamental computer algorithms that can be organized in the following algorithmic paradigms: dynamic programming, linear programming, greedy algorithms, divide-and-conquer algorithms, graph algorithms, intelligent search algorithms, and --to a lesser degree--randomized algorithms. As an algorithmic problem solver, the student can formulate novel or unfamiliar problems in mathematical terms so as to elucidate a given problem's relation to known problems, choose a paradigm for solving the problem, and design an algorithm to solve the problem.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Correctness
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: The student can prove the correctness of a subset of the algorithms considered in the course.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analysis
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: The student can analyze algorithms belonging to those algorithmic paradigms for their asymptotic worst-case behavior in terms of time and space. Furthermore, the student can analyze algorithms empirically (under simplifying assumptions) and understands the relationship of empirical analysis to average-case behavior.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Implementation
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: The student can independently implement selected algorithms in the context of motivating applications using Python.

subject: C S
title: Concepts of Programming Languages
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Principles and concepts characterizing high-level computer programming languages, process and data abstration, encapsulation, inheritance, functional programming, logic programming, scanners, and parsers.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Program Language Flexibility
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be able to write programs using non-imperative language paradigms.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Programming Language Familiarity
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn the vocabulary of programming language design, syntax, and semantics

subject: C S
title: Algorithmic Languages and Compilers
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Formal description of algorithmic languages and techniques used in their compilation:  semantics, ambiguities, procedures, replication, iteration, recursion.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Write a compiler
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Implement a fully functional compiler for a subset of the Java language that targets an embedded microprocess.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Use interesting data types and algorithms in a large program
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Apply patterns and common data structures to compiler constructrion including tree traversal, graph algorithms, and proof systems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Apply techniques from compiler construction to other problem
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students are able to synthesis algorithms and techniques from compiler constructor to other problem domains.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Mental model of compilation
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students have a sound mental model of how an abstract high level language is systematically transformed into a low-level machine langauge or byte code. Students are able to use that understanding to write better application code.

subject: C S
title: Software Engineering
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Analysis, design, implementation, and testing of significant software systems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Group Dynamics
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate the ability to work in a group on a significant project

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Software Engineering Fundamentals
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate practices that result in quality software

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Software Ethics
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate an understanding of how ethics impact software development

subject: C S
title: Operating Systems Design
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Principles and concepts of operating systems design and the implementation of an operating system.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Operating System Basics
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Describe the basic components of an operating system and their role in implementations for general purpose, real-time, and embedded applications.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Basic Concepts
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Define the concepts of processes, threads, asynchronous signals and competitive system resource allocation.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Shells
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Show how a shell works.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Scheduling Algorithms for Multi-Tasking
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Explain what multi-tasking is and outline standard scheduling algorithms for multi-tasking.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Mutual Exclusion Principles
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Discuss mutual exclusion principles and their use in concurrent programming, including semaphore construction and resource allocation.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: System Memory Management
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Give an overview of system memory management.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Implementing File Systems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Explain how file systems are implemented.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Competing Operating Systems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Discuss the features and strengths of various contemporary operating systems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Implement an Operating System
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Implement a functional, pre-emptive, multi-tasking operating system written in C, including a shell, scheduler, file manager, memory manager, and synchronization components.

subject: C S
title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to core areas of artifical intelligence; intelligent agents, problem solving and search, knowledge-based systems and inference, planning, uncertainty, learning, and perception.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Recognize problems that can use AI methods
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: from business, medicine, robotics, gaming, and machine learning

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Identify the component elements of the problem
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Does it require basic control?
Does it involve uncertain reasoning?
Does it have a simple goal, sophisticated utility, or multiple attributes?
Does it require sequential choice or planning?
How may decision makers are involved?

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Formalize the problem in a way that is amenable to a solutio
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
What is the internal representation?
What performance criteria are relevant?
What is the nature of the environment?
What are the actuators?
What are the sensors?
What are the agent's utilities?
How is uncertainty represented?

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Be able to analyze, implement, and experiment with several A
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Uninformed, informed, constraint-satisfaction, hill-climbing, and stochastic (e.g., genetic algorithms) search
Markov provesses, Bayes rule, Bayes nets, Hidden Markov Models, Grid Filters, and Kalman Filters
Expected utility theory
Multi-attribute utility theory
Sequential choice under uncertainty: value and policy iteration
Minimax solutions from game theory: alpha-beta pruning

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analyze and communicate solution quality
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Determine whether your solution is correct
Express the quality of your solution
Value the need to communicate your solution to others

subject: C S
title: Computer Graphics
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Interactive computer graphics systems programming and architecture.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Understanding fundamental principles of computer graphics
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be proficient in basic computer graphics algorithms such as modeling, trnasformations and rendering.Students will design and code solutions to several programming problems in graphics using algorithms and data structures.In addition, sutudents will use these techniques to program a large semester project.

subject: C S
title: Introduction to Computational Theory
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Finite state automata, regular languages, lexical analysis; push-down automata, context-free languages, parsing; Turing machines and unrestricted grammars; computability complexity, NP-completeness.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Understand Paradigms
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the difference between and limitations of paradigms.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Decidable and Tractable
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the difference between computable (decidable) and practically computable (tractable).

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Categorize Problems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Categorize problems into one of the existing paradigms.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Solutions to Problems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Design and justify solutions to problems of decidability and tractability.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Understand Computing Paradigms
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the three computing paradigms (DFAs, PDA, Turing Machine) and their language-based equivalents (regular languages,context-free languages, computable algorithms).

subject: C S
title: Computer Communications and Networking
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to data communications and computer networking.  Communications fundamentals, computer networks, software, architecture, telecommunications, regulation, standards.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Internet Architecture
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the principles of the Internet architecture

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Internet Design
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand how the Internet works, from the transport layer down to the physical layer

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Internet Implementation
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Gain hands-on experience with implementing and simulating transport, routing, and MAC protocols, leading to a deeper understanding of these critical Internet components

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Internet Research
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be capable of reading and understanding research papers in the area of computer networking

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Scholarly Communication
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate the ability to effectively conduct, describe and report on an experimental research project in the area of computer networking

subject: C S
title: Database Modeling Concepts
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Database models:  relational, deductive, object-oriented.  Integrity constraints, query languages, database design.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Query Languages
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be able to use a variety of commercial query languages (SQL, XQuery, SPARQL), both stand-alone and embeded in high-level programming languages. Know how relational algebra and relational calculus provide a basis for commercial query languages.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Query Optimization
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand performance issues and optimization strategies. This includes query rewriting, secondary storage characteristics, and access strategies.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Database Design
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be able to design and develop relational database applications. This includes conceptual modeling and normalization theory.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Current Challenges
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be familiar with some of the current challenges facing database professionals.

subject: C S
title: Advanced Software Construction
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Advanced software development with an object-oriented focus. Design, implementation, and testing of medium-sized programs including a server program.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Construction
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn to construct a medium-scale server program with data persistence in a principled way by applying relevant engineering techniques such as up-front design, quality code construction, unit testing, assertions, and error handling.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Design
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn and apply basic software design principles such as single responsibility, low coupling, avoidance of code duplication and information/data hiding to create modular code.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Databases
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn and apply the basics of relational database design, modeling and programmatic access.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Security
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn and apply basic security principles and concepts.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Testing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn to validate a program’s behavior against its specification using testing practices.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Tools
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use modern software tools including integrated development environments, testing frameworks, debuggers, version control, and documentation processing tools.

subject: C S
title: Computer Vision
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to fundamental concepts and algorithms of computer vision, including feature extraction, detection, segmentation, registration, recognition, motion, 3D vision, and image understanding. Applies techniques from image processing, geometry, Bayesian methods, optimization, and machine learning to computer vision problems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Introduction to Digital Signal and Image Processing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Understand and apply the following:

Sampling and quantization of image information
Camera properties and the acquisition of images
Mathematics of image-space transformations
Fourier transforms and filtering

subject: C S
title: Mobile and Ubiquitous Human-Computer Interaction
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Iterative user experience design for mobile and ubiquitous computing with an emphasis on conceptualization and prototyping in specific contexts. Complete an open-ended project from initial design through functional prototype, with design and critique sessions.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Needs analysis
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Characterize user needs in a specific context using inductive research methods

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Mobile sensors
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Implement mobile phone applications that use sensors found in a mobile phone

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Screenless applications
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Implement screenless applications using small portable computers

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Theory
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Discuss important issues in mobile and ubiquitous computing in the context of relevant theories.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Evaluation of prototypes
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Characterize strengths and weaknesses of an interactive system based on user-centric evaluation.

subject: C S
title: Large-Scale Distributed System Design
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Principles and concepts of designing and building distributed systems.  Introduction to architectures for distributed computation.  Reliability, availability, and scalability of large applications.  Cloud computing and APIs.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Computational Practice
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Students can implement programming projects that display knowledge of a variety of distributed system architectural styles. Some of these assignments represent significant programming projects with wide leeway in design and implementation choices.
Students will analyze problems, determine solutions within an assigned architectural style, and successfully implement those solutions.
Students will design and implement projects both individually and as part of a team.
Students will use cloud-based systems to run and implement assignments
Students will manage a cloud-based Web server and properly configure it.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Computational Theory
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Students will understand the proper use of distributed and decentralized architectures and algorithms including

RESTful APIs
Hierarchical vs heterarchical system architectures
MapReduce
Distributed Hash Tables
Event-driven system
Peer-to-peer architectures including Paxos, gossip protocols, and blockchain

Students will understand theoretical concepts and limitations of distributed systems including

network practicalities such as latency, bandwidth, topology, cost, heterogeneity, security
CAP theorem
distributed transactions
serialization
identity and naming
cryptography
simultaneity

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Critical Thinking and Communication
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will interact with peers in meaningful discussion, lead discussions in peer groups, prepare written reports, and make oral presentations.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Ethics and Computing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be presented with societal outcomes of technological choices in distributed and decentralized systems, analyze those outcomes, explore possible alternatives from different choices, and present their findings in small groups.

subject: C S
title: Introduction to Computer Systems
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: How a computer works to execute sequential code: low level data representation and abstraction, the relationship between C and assembly, computer architecture and pipelining, the memory hierarchy, dynamic memory allocation, and linking.\n

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Binary Computations
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Ability to perform simple computations in binary.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: C Programming
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Ability to program in C.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Assembly Programming
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Ability to program in assembly.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Translating C code to Assembly
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Ability to describe how a compiler translates C code to assembly.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Computer Operation
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Ability to describe the operation of a computer at the micro-architectural level

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Boolean Logic
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Perform computations using Boolean logic

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Translate Boolean
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Translate Boolean logic to transistor circuitry

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Circuitry
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use combinational and sequential circuitry to implement an instruction set architecture

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Computer Architecture
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Ability to describe the high level organization and operation of a computer, e.g. the von Neumann model

subject: C S
title: Ethics and Computers in Society
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Societal impact of computer technology, the computer scientist's place in society, ethical issues.  Reading, discussion, and writing seminar.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Ethics
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Analyze, write about, and discuss issues and ethical problems related to computing and the application of evolving technologies in society.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: History of Computing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Appreciate the history of computing as a means for students to sharpen their sense of their own future.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Continuing Education
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will understand the need for continuing education in their careers.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Presenting Ideas
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be able to effectively present their ideas.

subject: C S
title: Computer Security
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to computer security fundamentals: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and access control. Secret key and public key cryptography, network security protocols, viruses, and fire walls.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Build a system
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Implement a cryptographic algorithm from a standards specification.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Break and fix a system
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate how attackers compromise real-world systems, and then show how to prevent these attacks.

subject: C S
title: Introduction to Machine Learning
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Machine learning models and other mechanisms allowing computers to learn and find knowledge from data.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Recognize
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: You will be able to recognize when machine learning and data mining tools are applicable.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Execute
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: You will be able to plan and execute successful machine learning and data mining projects, including selecting an adequate process for your specific task and avoiding the main machine learning pitfalls.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Use Effective Machine Learning Techniques
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: You will learn the basic theory and models used in machine learning

subject: C S
title: Data Structures and Algorithms
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Fundamental data structures and algorithms of computer science; basic algorithm analysis; recursion; sorting and searching; lists, stacks, queues, trees, hashing; object-oriented data abstraction.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Data Computing Fundamentals
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use the fundamental data types of computing (lists, stacks, queues, priority queues, sets, maps, trees, etc.).

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Implementing Fundamental Data Types
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the major techniques for implementing the fundamental data types (linked lists, binary search trees, hashing, heaps, etc.) and implement several of them.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Select Data Structures
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Properly use and select data structures from language-provided data-structure libraries.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analysis
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Apply basic algorithm analysis.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Recursion
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand how recursion works and write programs using recursion to solve problems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Usage of Algorithms
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Make informed decisions about which sorting and searching algorithms to use in specific circumstances.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Program Writing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Write programs that require ~500 lines of code.

subject: C S
title: Discrete Structures
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to grammars and parsing; predicate and propositional logic; proof techniques; sets, functions, relations, relational data model; graphs and graph algorithms.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Write Code From Diverse Program Components
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate ability to build large programs by writing and integrating code from a diverse spectrum of program components.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Use Mathematical Structures to Solve Programming Problems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand and be conversant with basic discrete mathematical structures (finite state machines, regular expressions, grammars, propositional calculus, proof techniques, predicate calculus, sets, relations, functions, graphs).Write complex programs by using mathematical concepts as the basis for solving programming problems (finite state machines for lexical analysis; grammars for parsing; propositional and predicate calculus for logic programming; sets and algebras for relational databases; algebras, graphs, and topological sorting for optimizing datalog query processing).Incrementally build sophisticated programs by a systematic design process based on discrete mathematics.Design a solution for a programming problem and justify the design as one that is maintainable and extendable by other programmers who understand and are conversant with discrete mathematical structures.

subject: C S
title: Advanced Topics in Computer Science
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Advanced undergraduate- and graduate-level subjects as announced before each semester.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Advanced topic in Computer Science
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will learn a topic in Computer Science not covered by the regular curriculum.

subject: C S
title: Topics in Computer Science
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Undergraduate level subjects as announced before each semester.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Special Topics
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will learn about a topic in Computer Science not covered in the traditional courses.

subject: C S
title: Verification and Validation
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Foundational topics in verification and validation; the application of logic to building correct systems. Covers logics to formally specify properties of systems, verifications of basic systems, and advanced topics in formal verification for complex systems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Formally reason about concurrent systems and executions
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Formally reason about concurrent systems and executions

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Define correctness in concurrent systems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Define basic correctness principles of concurrent systems including deadlock, live-lock, safety, and general liveness.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analyze concurrent objects for correctness
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use tools to analyze concurrent objects for correctness and performance

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Learn best-practices for spin locks and monitors
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn best-practices for spin locks and monitors

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Discuss wait-free versus lock-free implementations
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Compare and contrast wait-free and lock-free properties in concurrent objects

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Understand and use concurrent data structures
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand and use concurrent data structures

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Correctly use barriers and fences
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Correctly use barriers and fences

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Have working knowledge of tools and techniques for testing c
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Have working knowledge of tools and techniques for testing concurrent programs

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Be familiar to libraries to support concurrent programming i
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be familiar to libraries to support concurrent programming in C, C , C#, and Java

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Describe future trends in multi-processor programming
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Describe future trends in multi-processor programming

subject: C S
title: Linear Programming and Convex Optimization
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Optimization, problem formulation, and solution algorithms, including simplex and interior point methods.  Applications from control, data mining, finance, game theory, learning, network flow, operations research, and statistical estimation.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Mathematical Foundations
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Develop a fluency in the mathematical foundations needed to pose optimization problems, including an appreciation for the role of convexity in characterizing solvable problems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Simplex Method
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Develop a fluency with the Simplex Method as a solution technique to Linear Programming problems. Understand how it exploits the linear nature of the problem to yield good average-case performance while failing to be efficient in the worst-case.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Sensitivity Analysis
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be able to characterize how to perturb the data of an existing problem so that its solution remains optimal for the new, perturbed problem.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Applications
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Explore the role of Linear and Convex Programming in a variety of applications, including 1) Finance, 2) Game Theory, 3) Regression, and 4) Computer Networking.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Interior Point Methods
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Develop a fluency with interior point methods for solving Linear Programming problems and understand how these solutions may be extended to solve nonlinear, convex optimization problems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Duality Theory
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the meaning of weak and strong duality and their role in the design and verification of algorithmic solutions to optimization problems.

subject: C S
title: Creating and Managing a Software Business
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Entrepreneurship, idea/opportunity generation, strategic planning, legal organization, product development, marketing/sales, customer support, fund raising, and effective management.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Business Plan
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will write a business plan for a company they are proposing.

subject: C S
title: Topics in Computer Science
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Undergraduate-level topics as announced.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Data gathering
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Because 301R is an experiment course, data on how well the course accomplished the outcomes will be gathered.  This will usually be in the form of surveys, exam scores, and project scores.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Experimental course
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: A 301R course is used by a professor who is teaching an experimental course.  Learning outcomes will vary for the course, but these outcomes are evaluated during the vetting process (through the department undergraduate curriculum committee) before the professor is allowed to teach the course.  At this level, typical learning outcomes involve computing practice, critical thinking, and more advanced theory.

subject: C S
title: Software Design
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Use design, development, testing and refactoring techniques to build and evolve reliable, maintainable and scalable software systems. Covers a wide range of design patterns and principles. Also introduces students to software architecture and architectural patterns.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Design Judgement
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Know when and how to apply various design patterns.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Software Design Principles
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the principles of effective software design.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Document and Communicate Designs
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn to effectively document and communicate software designs.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Cloud Computing Technologies
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Gain experience implementing software designs using cloud computing technologies.

subject: C S
title: Robust Control
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to the analysis and design of feedback systems guaranteed to perform well in spite of model uncertainty.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: LMIs
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand Linear Matrix Inequalities and their role in various identification and control problems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Systems Concepts
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand system concepts of stability, controllability, observability, minimality, behavior, and structure.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Representation
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand various mathematical representations of systems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Four Basic Problems
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the four basic problems: Model Reduction (i.e. Approximation), System Identification (i.e. Learning), Control (i.e. Decision Making), and Verification/Validation, and how they impact each other.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Hankel Singular Values
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand Hankel singular values and their role in balanced truncation as an approximation to optimal model reduction.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Small Gain
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the role of the Small Gain Theorem and Passivity in providing guarantees of algorithm correctness in spite of model uncertainty.

subject: C S
title: Fundamentals of Information Retrieval
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Concepts and terminology of information retrieval (IR) systems.  Design methodologies and issues.  Fundamental IR models examined:  Boolean, Vector Space, Probabilistic models, and evaluation strategies.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Understand and evaluate fundamental IR models
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Boolean
Vector Space
Probabilistic

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Query Construction
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Represent user's information needs, refine user's queries, and construct query results using snippets and categories

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Query Processing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Extract, parse, index, and classify source information for query processing

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Machine Learning
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Categorize, cluster, and filter query results using various supervised and unsupervised learning strategies

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analysis
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Evaluate the effectiveness, efficiency, and cost of performing searches using search engines

subject: C S
title: (C S-Chem-Geol-Math-MthEd-Phscs-Stat) Business Career Essentials in Science and Math
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction for science, math, and statistics majors to careers in industry. Project planning, oral and written business presentations, business accounting, and technology readiness.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Project Management
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn the basics of project management and understand why this is important to business success

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Business Presentations and Writing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Learn the basics of effective business presentations and writing

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Technology Readiness
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the different levels of technology readiness and the process of moving a project through these levels to completion

subject: C S
title: Advanced Techniques in Human Computer Interaction
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: This class combines designing the user experience with implementing the technology. Students learn website design, information architecture, and how to design for the broader ecosystem of use. Finally, students will learn principles specifically relevant to website design and information architecture. Web design is one of the most common UX jobs and so this makes their skills highly marketable.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analyze the Context of a Design Problem
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will learn how to analyze secondary data related to their design problem in order to understand the context in which people are using a given technology. Namely, this class goes beyond just interviewing users and to analyzing multiple sources of data that helps them understand the context in which the users are using the technology. This could include analyzing bug report, comments on social media, Q&A forums, other documentation about the topic, interview transcripts from multiple sets of stakeholders (e.g., users v. non users).

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Design the Different Stakeholders.
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be able to apply methods such as Personas to represent multiple stakeholders who are affected by the technology. This will allow them to analyze the characteristics of and segment the target population.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Apply Advanced Design Principles
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand and apply design patterns and theories of information to the design of an online systerm.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Implement Functional Prototype
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be able to specify and implement/create a functional prototype that improves on the user experience of the design problem.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Evaluate Design Improvement
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be able to compare different designs to determine which has a better user experience using techniques such as A/B testing. This could be comparing the existing website that they have been analyzing, with a newly proposed/implemented website design for the same organization.

subject: C S
title: Interactive Graphics and Image Processing
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduces basic concepts of computer graphics and image processing. Includes characteristics of physical cameras and displays; color models; basic image processing algorithms: 2D and 3D homogeneous coordinate transformations; fundamentals of 3D rendering geometry; lighting and shading: image warping and texture mapping; and frequency-domain processing.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Mathematics and Geometry
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Use and implement the fundamental mathematical concepts underlying these areas, including

Points, vectors, and matrices
Other basic principles of linear algebra
Geometric transformations as matrices
Methods for working with numeric data

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Graphical User Interfaces
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Use and implement concepts of current graphical user interfaces (which you should have seen before), including

Model-view-controller architecture
Event handling
Screen management

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: 2D Drawing, Viewing, and Selection
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Use and implement the following concepts for 2D drawing, viewing, and selection:

Geometry of simple shapes
2D Transformations
Transformation composition

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: 3D Rendering Geometry
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Understand and implement the fundamental geometry involved in 3-D perspective rendering, including

Use rendering libraries such as OpenGL to do simple rendering
Implement the geometric transformations used in 3D rendering
Identify basic techniques for shading and their advantages and limitations

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Basic Image Processing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective:
Understand and implement fundamental image processing operations, including 

Point operations (brightness, contrast, etc.)
Image-Image operations (differencing, alpha blending, etc.)
Neighborhood operations (noise removal, edge detection, etc.)

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Geometric Operations on Images
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand and implement image warping using a backwards-mapping approach

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Color
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understandand use the language and principles of common color models

subject: C S
title: Undergraduate Special Projects
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Students identify an interesting problem in computer science and solve it with the aid of faculty members.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Capstone Experience in a Special Topic
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will propose, execute, and report a capstone project in an area that they choose in consultation with a professor. Each project is approved by the Undergraduate Curriculum Committee.

subject: C S
title: Topics in Computer Science
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Undergraduate level subjects as announced before each semester.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Data gathering
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Because 201R is an experiment course, data on how well the course accomplished the outcomes will be gathered.  This will usually be in the form of surveys, exam scores, and project scores.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Experimental course
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: A 201R course is used by a professor who is teaching an experimental course.  Learning outcomes will vary for the course, but these outcomes are evaluated during the vetting process (through the department undergraduate curriculum committee) before the professor is allowed to teach the course.  At this level, typical learning outcomes involve computing practice and fundamental theory.

subject: C S
title: Web Programming
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to building and deploying full stack web applications using core technologies, frameworks, protocols, and development tools.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Web Pages
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Create interactive web applications using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Web Frameworks
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Modularize, build, and package an application using a web framework.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Web Services
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Create a backend service using DNS, HTTPS, WebSocket, service endpoints, authentication, and data persistence.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Infrastructure
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Deploy applications and manage code.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Security and Design
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Appreciate production web application security and design practices.

subject: C S
title: Systems Programming
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Systems programming principles and concepts, including Linux systems programming, multiprocessing, concurrency, exceptional control flow, caching, sockets, protocols, and non-blocking I/O.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Demonstrate Linux Systems Programming
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use Linux APIs and command line

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Demonstrate multiprocessing, concurrency and exceptional control flow
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Write programs that use concurrency

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analyze caching, sockets and non-blocking I/O
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Write programs and analyze their performance

subject: C S
title: Capstone 1
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, test, and demonstrate a major project.
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 CS GPA or instructor's consent.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will design and implement software
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.

subject: C S
title: Capstone 2
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: The second semester of a culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, test, and demonstrate a major project.
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 CS GPA or instructor's consent.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will design and implement software
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.

subject: C S
title: Undergraduate Research
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Students receive course credit for doing undergraduate research under the supervision of a faculty mentor.
prereqText: Students must turn in signed research agreement at least 1 business day before add/drop deadline in order to receive permission-to-add code. Research agreement form can be found at http://cs.byu.edu or in the CS Department office.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will gain research skills
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students work in a laboratory and develop research skills

subject: C S
title: Computing Competitions
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Students work in teams to prepare for and compete in computer science competitions in areas such as programming, data science, and ethical hacking.
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 C S GPA or instructor's consent.

subject: C S
title: Secondary Minor Student Teaching
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Supervised, full-day, four week experience to develop and demonstrate competence in teaching minor area course content in the secondary classroom.
prereqText: Sc Ed 477R or minor area equivalent; admittance to teaching area; FBI fingerprint background clearance.

subject: C S
title: Quality Assurance and Developer Operations
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Comprehensive production software testing, deployment, and management strategies, focusing on automation, security, quality assurance, continuous integration/deployment, scalability, and resilience.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Quality
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Implement comprehensive automated testing strategies, including unit, UI, E2E, performance, and load testing.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Deployment
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Deploy various automated CI/CD pipeline strategies to reliably deliver software that is resilient, scalable, and secure.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Management
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Monitor, detect anomalies, alter, and automatically correct system behavior.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Curious
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Characterize and address gaps in understanding related to DevOps techniques and principles.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Creative
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Discuss and generate alternatives DevOps designs.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Collaborative
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Work as a team to discover DevOps design weaknesses.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Christlike
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Strive for divine inspiration for, and ability to apply capabilities to, celestial pursuits.

subject: C S
title: Introduction to Deep Learning
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Understand theory and practice of deep learning, drawing material from machine vision, machine translation, dynamical systems, audio processing, neural computing and human perception. Learn supporting mathematical concepts, including linear algebra, stochastic optimization, and hardware acceleration.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analyze Deep Learning
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Theory and practice of modern deep learning and associated software frameworks. A broad look at the field, drawing on material from machine vision, machine translation, dynamical systems, audio processing, neural computing and human perception. Supporting mathematical concepts are also covered, including linear algebra, stochastic optimization, and hardware acceleration.

subject: C S
title: Software Engineering Lab 1
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: The first of three experiential learning labs that will provide students with hands-on experience with various tools, technologies, and techniques that software engineers use in practice. In addition to hands-on lab experiences, this course contains lecture and assigned reading content that will provide an understanding of what a software engineering career is and what roles and responsibilities are available to software engineers.
prereqText: With instructor consent, may be taken concurrently with C S 203 and 204.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Definition and roles
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Know what software engineering is. Know the different roles within a software engineering career.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: UI Design
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use a modeling tool to create wireframe and screenflow diagrams to represent a UI design.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Modeling
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Create and understand UML Class, Sequence and Activity diagrams.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Code Reviews
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand what code reviews are and why they are useful.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Documentation
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the importance of software documentation and use a tool to generate code level documentation.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Source Control
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand why source control is needed and gain experience using a source control tool.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Coding Style
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Know how to use tools to enforce coding style decisions.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Debugging
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Be able to debug software programs, both from the command line and from an IDE using a debugger.

subject: C S
title: Software Engineering Lab 2
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: The second of three experiential learning labs that will provide students with hands-on experience with various tools, technologies, and techniques that software engineers use in practice. The lab experiences in this course build on those learned in CS 202 and focus on tools and techniques for source code testing, automated source code analysis, and source code build and dependency management.
prereqText: With instructor consent, may be taken concurrently with C S 202 and 204.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Analysis
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use static analysis tools to identify and correct potential code problems.  Understand what code profiling is and use a code profiler to identify performance trouble spots in a software program.  Use a testing tool to test application performance.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Management
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Demonstrate how to use build and dependency management tools to manage a large, complex code base.  Link to Program Outcomes: None

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Testing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the basic concepts and approaches used by software engineers to test software.  Use a testing framework to write automated unit tests.  Use a code coverage tool to assess code coverage.

subject: C S
title: Software Engineering Lab 3
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: The third of three experiential learning labs that will provide students with hands-on experience with various tools, technologies, and techniques that software engineers use in practice. The lab experiences in this course build on those learned in CS 202 and CS 203 and focus on tools and techniques for cloud development and Agile programming.
prereqText: With instructor consent, may be taken concurrently with C S 202 and 203.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Virtual Machines
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Set up virtual machines and application containers to run applications for different operating systems on a single host.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Cloud Computing
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Provision a simple web server on AWS.  Gain experience with AWS services.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Deployment
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Use a tool to build a simple deployment pipeline.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Dependency Injection
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Understand the benefits of dependency injection.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Object-relational Mapping
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Gain experience with an object-relational mapping framework.

subject: C S
title: Software Engineering Capstone 1
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.\n
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 CS GPA or instructor's consent.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will design and implement software
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.

subject: C S
title: Software Engineering Capstone 2
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 CS GPA or instructor's consent.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will design and implement software
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.

subject: C S
title: Data Science Capstone 1
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 CS GPA or instructor's consent.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will design and implement a data science solution
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.

subject: C S
title: Data Science Capstone 2
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.
prereqText: Minimum 3.0 CS GPA or instructor's consent.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Students will design and implement a data science solution
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Culminating experience based on skills learned in advanced technical courses. Students work in teams to plan, design, implement, test, and demonstrate a major project.

subject: C S
title: Job Search Strategies
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Course will assist graduating students in identifying and connecting to potential employers.

subject: C S
title: Introduction to Data Science
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: This course is a broad, interdisciplinary look at the field of data science, and how to derive insight from data.  It will develop technical skills (including some python programming, statistics, linear algebra, machine learning, data cleaning and visualization) as well as data literacy (mental frameworks for decomposing data science problems, critical thinking about potential conclusions of an analysis, and potential pitfalls of overreliance on unreliable data).
prereqText: CS 110 or equivalent prior programming experience

subject: C S
title: Collaborative Problem Solving
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Strengthen problem-solving skills through targeted practice to prepare for technical/coding interviews (a key challenge when job interviewing). Building on techniques from CS 312, learn to collaborate and quickly identify and apply appropriate strategies to problems.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Demonstrate problem solving
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will be able to solve problems quickly to enable them to be competitive in technical lnterviews

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Build problem-solving strategies
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Students will build on the algorithms and problem-solving strategies that they were taught and had limited practice with in CS 312. learning primarily through targeted practice.

subject: C S
title: Voice User Interfaces
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Methods and development platforms for Voice User Interfaces. Principles of effective design; differences from visual or menu-based interfaces’ mechanics of automatic speech recognition, intent detection, slot filling, dialog state tracking, text generation, and knowledge representation.

expectedLearningOutcomes.name: Develop a voice interface for a program
expectedLearningOutcomes.objective: Methods and development platforms for Voice User Interfaces. Principles of effective design; differences from visual or menu-based interfaces' mechanics of automatic speech recognition, intent detection, slot filling, dialog state tracking, text generation, and knowledge representation.

subject: C S
title: Theory of Predictive Modeling
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Mathematical, computational, and philosophical foundations of machine learning, control, and physical modeling.  Introduction to system identification, causality, uncertainty, model approximation, and information geometry.

subject: C S
title: How to Program
college: College of Computational, Mathematical, & Physical Sciences
description: Introduction to programming and computer science for those with no prior programming experience. Focuses on the basic aspects of programming, with an emphasis on core principles. Labs provide a supportive environment to learn how to program with peers. Weekly discussions will introduce some of the many ways computer science interacts with society. Programming assignments will demonstrate how to use computing in a variety of disciplines. \n
